dt.t: The {wi}dinosaurs{/wi}, which once dominated the earth, disappeared very swiftly, leaving room for tiny shrewlike creatures to crawl out of shelter and start on the road to mammalian domination of the planet.

dt: Dinosaurs have traditionally been considered a separate group from birds, which evolved from dinosaurs, but modern paleontologists now view birds as survivors of a {d_link|theropod|theropod} lineage of dinosaurs. In this classification, all dinosaurs except birds became extinct at the end of the Cretaceous period approximately 65 million years ago, with all dinosaurs that are not birds referred to as {it}dinosaurs{/it} or {i_link|non-avian dinosaurs|non-avian dinosaur} and birds typically referred to as {i_link|avian dinosaurs|avian dinosaur}.

et: borrowed from presumed New Latin {it}*dinosaurus,{/it} the base of {it}Dinosauria,{/it} former reptile taxon, from Greek {it}deinós{/it} {ldquo}inspiring dread or awe{rdquo} + {it}-o-{/it} {et_link|-o-|-o-} + New Latin {it}Sauria,{/it} former reptile suborder, from Greek {it}saúros{/it} "lizard" + New Latin {it}-ia{/it} {et_link|-ia:2|-ia:2} {ma}{mat|deinonychus|}, {mat|-saurus|}{/ma}

The taxonomic name {it}Dinosauria{/it} as well as the vernacular form {it}dinosaur{/it} were both introduced by the British biologist and paleontologist Richard Owen (1804-92) in {ldquo}Report on British Fossil Reptiles. Part II,{rdquo} {it}Report of the Eleventh Meeting of the British Association for the Advancement of Science{/it} ({ldquo}Held at Plymouth in July 1841{rdquo}) (London: J. Murray, 1842), p. 103: {ldquo}The combination of such characters, some, as the sacral ones [i.e., the sacral vertebrae fused into a single structure], altogether peculiar among Reptiles, others borrowed, as it were, from groups now distinct from each other, and all manifested by creatures far surpassing in size the largest of existing reptiles, will, it is presumed, be deemed sufficient ground for establishing a distinct tribe or sub-order of Saurian Reptiles, for which I would propose the name of {it}Dinosauria.{/it} [Footnote to the above] Gr. {it}deinòs,{/it} fearfully great; {it}saúros,{/it} a lizard.{rdquo} Although Owen's {ldquo}Report on British Fossil Reptiles{rdquo} purports to be the record of an oral presentation given at Plymouth in July, 1841, the detailed contemporary accounts of Owen's lecture do not mention {it}dinosaur{/it} or {it}Dinosauria,{/it} and it is now clear that he only introduced the word with the extensively revised version of the report released in April, 1842. The background to Owen's report is analyzed by Hugh S. Torrens in {ldquo}Politics and Paleontology: Richard Owen and the Invention of Dinosaurs,{rdquo} M. K. Brett-Surman, et al., editors, {it}The Complete Dinosaur,{/it} 2nd edition (Indiana University Press, 2012), pp. 25-43. Offprints of Owen's article have the publication date 1841, but, as Torrens demonstrates (p. 34), this was an error (perhaps deliberately uncorrected by Owen). Owen's rendering of Greek {it}deinós{/it} (a word with a wide semantic range) as {ldquo}fearfully great{rdquo} is at odds with the conventional notion that {it}dinosaur{/it} means {ldquo}terrible lizard{rdquo} in Greek. Although {ldquo}terrible{rdquo} (i.e., terrifying) is a possible translation of {it}deinós,{/it} it does not appear to be the meaning Owen intended.
